text: The robotic surgery reveals a large direct right inguinal hernia sac. The surgeon begins dissection medially, just lateral to the medial umbilical ligament, working from medial to lateral. The peritoneal lining and transverse fascia are kept thin laterally, allowing fat to remain on the abdominal wall. The dissection extends far medially to expose Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, then proceeds laterally along the ileopubic tract above the myopeptinial orifice.

The surgeon manipulates the large direct inguinal hernia sac, pushing it up and away with counter traction and electrocautery, causing it to fall away. The spermatic cord and vas deferens are visible as the surgeon navigates around them. A small femoral hernia plug is also noted, being carefully dissected off the right iliac region.
The surgeon identifies a small cord lipoma during the medial dissection, extending the dissection towards the pubic tubercle and near the symphysis pubis medially.

The surgeon places a large barred 3D max mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it is displaced medially beneath the pubic tubercle to prevent recurrence. The mesh is sutured to the hernia sac, medial aspect of the defect, Cooper's ligament, and pubic tubercle. The surgeon performs a high lateral fixation, carefully maneuvering the needle through the tissue.
The surgeon closes the flap using a one-handed running suture with PDS. The hernia defect is sutured back onto the flap closure site to reinforce the repair and reduce the risk of recurrence.
